elements.sent-2411-001: We propose and analyze a space--time Local Discontinuous Galerkin method for the approximation of the solution to parabolic problems.
elements.sent-2411-002: The method allows for very general discrete spaces and prismatic space--time meshes.
elements.sent-2411-003: Existence and uniqueness of a discrete solution are shown by means of an inf-sup condition, whose proof does not rely on polynomial inverse estimates.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, for piecewise polynomial spaces satisfying an additional mild condition, we show a second inf-sup condition that provides an additional control of the time derivative of the discrete solution.
elements.sent-2411-005: We derive~$hp$-a priori error bounds based on these inf-sup conditions, which we use to prove convergence rates for standard, tensor-product, and quasi-Trefftz polynomial spaces.
elements.sent-2411-006: Numerical experiments validate our theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-007: Space--time finite element method, Local Discontinuous Galerkin method, inf-sup stability, parabolic problem, prismatic space--time meshes.
elements.sent-2411-008: We are interested in the numerical approximation of the solution to a parabolic problem on a space--time cylinder~$\QT = \Omega \times (0, T)$, where~$\Omega$ is an open, bounded polytopic domain in~$\IR^d$ ($d \in \{1,2, 3\}$) with Lipschitz boundary~$\partial \Omega$, and~$T > 0$ is some final time.
elements.sent-2411-009: Let~$\bk$ be a symmetric positive definite diffusion tensor such that, for some constant~$\theta > 0$, \begin{equation}\label{EQN::DIFFUSION} \by^T \bk \by \geq \theta |\by|^2 \qquad \forall \by \in \IR^d,\end{equation} and let~$f: \QT \rightarrow \IR$, $u_0 :\Omega \rightarrow \IR$, and $\gD : \partial \Omega \times (0, T) \rightarrow \IR$ be prescribed source term, initial datum, and Dirichlet boundary datum, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-010: The considered IBVP reads: find~$u : \QT \rightarrow \IR$ such that \begin{subequations \begin{alignat {3 \dpt u - \nablax \cdot (\bk \nablax u) = f \qquad \text{ in \QT, u = \gD \qquad \text{ on \partial \Omega \times (0, T), u = u_0 \qquad \text{ on \Omega \times \{0\}.. \end{alignat \end{subequations.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: For initial datum~$u_0 \in L^2(\Omega)$, source term~$f \in L^2(0, T; H^{-1 (\Omega))$, and homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions~$(\gD = 0)$, we define the following spaces: \begin{equation}Y := L^2(0, T; H_0^1(\Omega)) \quad \text{ and } \quad X:= Y \cap H^1(0, T; H^{-1}(\Omega)),\end{equation} and their associated norms \begin{equation}\Norm{v}{Y}^2 := \int_0^T \Norm{\nablax v(\cdot, t)}{L^2(\Omega)^d}^2 \dt \quad \text{ and } \quad \Norm{v}{X}^2 := \frac12 \Norm{v(\cdot, T)}{L^2(\Omega)}^2 + \theta \Norm{v}{Y}^2 + \Norm{\dpt v}{L^2(0, T; H^{-1}(\Omega))}^2,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\Norm{\varphi}{L^2(0, T; H^{-1}(\Omega))} := \sup_{0 \neq v \in Y} \frac{\langle \varphi, v\rangle}{\Norm{v}{Y}} \qquad \qquad \forall \varphi \in L^2(0, T; H^{-1}(\Omega)),\end{equation} with~$\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ denoting the duality between~$L^2(0, T; H^{-1 (\Omega))$ and~$Y$.}}

elements.sent-2411-015: Space--time finite element methods treat time as an additional space dimension in a time-dependent PDE, which results in many advantages, such as simultaneous high-order accuracy in space and time, the possibility of performing space--time adaptive refinements, the natural treatment of problems on moving domains, and suitability to be combined with parallel-in-time solvers.
elements.sent-2411-016: Motivated by such advantages, in the literature, several methods have been designed for the discretization of parabolic problems; see, e.g., the recent survey in~\cite{Langer_Steinbach_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, space--time methods related to the Petrov--Galerkin weak formulation in~\eqref{EQN::CONTINUOUS-WEAK-FORMULATION include conforming finite element~\cite{Aziz_Monk_1989,Steinbach_2015 , interior-penalty DG~\cite{Cangiani_Dong_Georgoulis:2017 , and virtual element~\cite{Gomez_Mascotto_Moiola_Perugia_2024,Gomez_Mascotto_Perugia_2024 methods.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Alternative approaches, such as wavelet~\cite{Schwab_Stevenson_2009 and finite element~\cite{Andreev_2013,Stevenson_Westerdiep_2021 methods based on a minimal residual Petrov--Galerkin formulation, least-squares methods~\cite{Bochev_Gunzburger_1998,Fuhrer_Karkulik_2021,Gantner_Stevenson_2024 , a discontinuous Petrov--Galerkin method~\cite{Diening_Storn_2022 , an isogeometric method with time-upwind test functions~\cite{Langer_Moore_Stephen_Neumuller_2016 , and a coercive formulation based on a Hilbert transformation of the test functions~\cite{Steinbach_Zank_2020 have also been considered.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: We focus on discontinuous Galerkin (DG) methods, which offer great flexibility in the choice of the discrete spaces and meshes that can be employed.
elements.sent-2411-020: In particular, the Local Discontinuous Galerkin (LDG) method~\cite{Cockburn_Shu:1998 has shown better stability properties than other DG methods (see, e.g., the comparative study of DG methods for elliptic PDEs in~\cite{Castillo_2002 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-021: We can reduce the number of degrees of freedom while keeping the approximation properties of full polynomial spaces by using special discrete spaces.
elements.sent-2411-022: Trefftz spaces are based on solutions of the homogeneous PDE and they have been used in the context of space--time DG methods.
elements.sent-2411-023: In particular, for wave problems in one space dimension see \cite{SpaceTimeTDG,KSTW2014,PFT09 , the acoustic wave equation \cite{PSSW20,Moiola_Perugia_2018,bgl2016 , elasto-acoustics \cite{bcds20 , time-dependent Maxwell's equation~\cite{EKSW15 , and the linear Schr\"odinger equation~\cite{Gomez_Moiola:2024,Gomez_Moiola_Perugia_Stocker:2023 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The uneven degree of the heat operator in different dimensions presents an interesting challenge in the construction of Trefftz spaces.
elements.sent-2411-025: Quasi-Trefftz spaces consists of functions that are only approximated solutions to the considered PDE.
elements.sent-2411-026: A general approach to construct suitable quasi-Trefftz spaces for general linear operators is presented in~\cite{IG_Moiola_Perinati_Stocker:2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: A method that avoids the explicit construction of Trefftz spaces is the embedded Trefftz method, see~\cite{LS_IJMNE_2023,Lozinski_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Numerical results of the embedded Trefftz method for the heat equation, and the possible choices for the Trefftz-like space, have been discussed in~\cite{ma_ch .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this work, we present and analyze a space--time LDG method for the discretization of parabolic problems.
elements.sent-2411-030: Below, we list the main theoretical and computational advantages of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-031: The method is well posed for very general prismatic space--time meshes and discrete spaces.
elements.sent-2411-032: We show two different inf-sup conditions.
elements.sent-2411-033: The first one is valid for any choices of the discrete spaces satisfying a local compatibility condition and does not rely on polynomial inverse estimates.
elements.sent-2411-034: The second one holds for polynomial spaces that satisfy also a local inclusion condition and provides an additional control on the first-order time derivative of the discrete solution; see~Section~\ref{SECT::WELL-POSEDNESS for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We derive~$hp$-a priori error bounds in some energy norms.
elements.sent-2411-036: Moreover, we prove $hp$-error estimates for standard and tensor-product polynomial spaces, and~$h$-error estimates for quasi-Trefftz spaces.
elements.sent-2411-037: The latter space allows for a significant reduction of the number of degrees of freedom; see Section~\ref{SECT::QUASI-TREFFTZ .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Optimal convergence rates of order~$\mathcal{O (h^{p+1 )$ are numerically observed for the error in the~$L^2(\QT)$-norm when standard piecewise polynomials of maximum degree~$p$ are used.}}

elements.sent-2411-291: We introduced a space--time Local Discontinuous Galerkin method for the discretization of the heat equation.
elements.sent-2411-292: The method is well posed in any space dimension~$d\in \{1, 2, 3\}$, and for very general prismatic space--time meshes and discrete spaces, even if polynomial inverse estimates are not available.
elements.sent-2411-293: Moreover, for piecewise polynomial spaces satisfying an additional mild condition, we showed an inf-sup condition that provides an additional control of the time derivative of the discrete solution.
elements.sent-2411-294: We have also derived~$hp$-a priori error bounds in some energy norms, and proven~$hp$-error estimates for standard and tensor-product polynomial spaces, and~$h$-error estimates for quasi-Trefftz spaces.
elements.sent-2411-295: In the numerical experiments presented, we have observed optimal convergence rates of order~$\mathcal{O (h^{p )$ for the error in the energy norms, and of order~$\mathcal{O (h^{p+1 )$ for the error in the~$L^2(\QT)$-norm for the four choices of discrete spaces presented: tensor-product, standard, quasi-Trefftz, and embedded Trefftz polynomial spaces.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-296: The two latter spaces allow for a significant reduction of the number of degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-297: We also have assessed the performance of the~$hp$-version of the method for some singular solutions.

elements.sent-2411-341: We are interested in the numerical approximation of the solution to a parabolic problem on a space--time cylinder~$\QT = \Omega \times (0, T)$, where~$\Omega$ is an open, bounded polytopal domain in~$\IR^d$ ($d \in \{1,2, 3\}$) with Lipschitz boundary~$\partial \Omega$, and~$T > 0$ is some final time.
elements.sent-2411-342: The considered initial and boundary value problem (IBVP) reads: find~$u : \QT \rightarrow \IR$ such that \begin{subequations \begin{alignat {3 \dpt u - \nablax \cdot (\bk \nablax u) = f \qquad \text{ in \QT, u = \gD \qquad \text{ on \partial \Omega \times (0, T), u = u_0 \qquad \text{ on \Omega \times \{0\}.. \end{alignat \end{subequations.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-343: Since the proposed method is nonconforming, additional regularity is required for the source term~$f$.
elements.sent-2411-344: More precisely, we henceforth assume that~$f \in L^2(\QT)$.
elements.sent-2411-345: In particular, space--time methods related to the Petrov--Galerkin weak formulation in~\eqref{EQN::CONTINUOUS-WEAK-FORMULATION include conforming finite element~\cite{Aziz_Monk_1989,Steinbach_2015 , interior-penalty discontinuous Galerkin~\cite{Cangiani_Dong_Georgoulis:2017 , and virtual element~\cite{Gomez_Mascotto_Moiola_Perugia_2024,Gomez_Mascotto_Perugia_2024 methods.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-346: We can reduce the number of degrees of freedom (DoFs) while keeping the approximation properties of full polynomial spaces by using special discrete spaces.
elements.sent-2411-347: Trefftz spaces are based on solutions to the homogeneous PDE and they have been used in the context of space--time DG methods.
elements.sent-2411-348: In particular, for wave problems in one space dimension~\cite{SpaceTimeTDG,KSTW2014,PFT09 , the acoustic wave equation~\cite{PSSW20,Moiola_Perugia_2018,bgl2016 , elasto-acoustics~\cite{bcds20 , time-dependent Maxwell's equations~\cite{EKSW15 , and the linear Schr\"odinger equation~\cite{Gomez_Moiola:2024,Gomez_Moiola_Perugia_Stocker:2023 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-349: A general approach to construct suitable quasi-Trefftz spaces for linear operators is presented in~\cite{IG_Moiola_Perinati_Stocker:2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-350: Numerical results of the embedded Trefftz method for the heat equation, and possible choices for the Trefftz-like space, have been discussed in~\cite{ma_ch .}

elements.sent-2411-353: Below, we list the main theoretical and computational results in this work.
elements.sent-2411-354: We establish the well-posedness of the method for very general prismatic space--time meshes and discrete spaces.
elements.sent-2411-355: We show two different inf-sup conditions, which require only that the stability parameter be strictly positive.
elements.sent-2411-356: The first one is valid for any choice of the discrete spaces satisfying a local compatibility condition and does not rely on polynomial inverse estimates.
elements.sent-2411-357: The second one holds for polynomial spaces that also satisfy a local inclusion condition and provides additional control on the first-order time derivative of the discrete solution; see~Section~\ref{SECT::WELL-POSEDNESS for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-358: Optimal convergence rates of order~$\mathcal{O (h^{p+1 )$ (where~$h$ denotes the maximum diameter of the space--time prismatic elements) are numerically observed for the error in the~$L^2(\QT)$ norm when standard piecewise polynomials of uniform degree~$p$ are used.}}
elements.sent-2411-359: Our stability and convergence results are analogous to those obtained in~\cite{Cangiani_Dong_Georgoulis:2017 for the space--time interior-penalty DG method with standard polynomial spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-360: However, in our analysis, we allow for prismatic meshes with hanging~time-like facets (see Figure~\ref{fig:hanging-time-like-facets and the notation introduced in Section~\ref{SECT::MESH-NOTATION below), which naturally arise when using local time steps.}}
elements.sent-2411-361: In particular, we show that stability and a priori error estimates can be established for elements with an arbitrary number of both space-like and~time-like facets.
elements.sent-2411-362: Moreover, as in the elliptic case, the proposed LDG method avoids the typical requirement of primal DG methods (such as the interior-penalty DG method) of a ``sufficiently large" stability parameter, which can have a negative impact on the conditioning of the associated matrix (see, e.g., \cite[\S4.2]{Castillo_2002 ).}
elements.sent-2411-363: Finally, we highlight that the LDG method for polytopal meshes has previously been analyzed only in~\cite{Ye_Zhang_Zhu:2022 (for elliptic problems).}
elements.sent-2411-364: Furthermore, some results from the present analysis can be adapted to study the~$hp$ version of the LDG method for elliptic problems on a broader class of polytopal meshes.
elements.sent-2411-365: In particular, $H^0(\Upsilon) := L^2(\Upsilon)$ is the space of Lebesgue square integrable functions over~$\Upsilon$, and~$H_0^1(\Upsilon)$ is the closure of~$C_0^{\infty (\Upsilon)$ in the~$H^1(\Upsilon)$ norm.}
elements.sent-2411-366: In Section~\ref{SECT::CONVERGENCE-ANALYSIS , we derive~a priori error bounds in some discrete energy norms, which are used to derive~a priori error estimates for different discrete polynomial spaces in Section~\ref{SECT::DISCRETE-SPACES .}}

elements.sent-2411-408: Via the embedded procedure, the discrete space $\VpK(K)$ is chosen as a weak Trefftz space as follows: \begin{equation}\label{DEF::ETK} \VpK(K) = \eT^p(K) :=\{v\in \Pp{p}{K}\, : \, \Pi^{p-2} (\mathcal{H} v) = 0 \text{ in } K\},\end{equation} where~$\Pi^{p-2}$ is the~$L^2(K)$-orthogonal projection operator in the space~$\Pp{p - 2 {K}$, and~$\mathcal{H}$ is as in the previous section.}
elements.sent-2411-409: The choice of the projection operator is crucial for the approximation properties of the embedded Trefftz space.
elements.sent-2411-410: Different choices for this operator have been discussed in~\cite[Ch.~3]{ma_ch .}
elements.sent-2411-411: The choice made here is heuristic; however, in the numerical results in Section~\ref{SECT::NUMERICAL-EXPERIMETNS below, we observe optimal convergence rates and the same reduction of the number of degrees of freedom as for the quasi-Trefftz space, i.e., the same space dimension for~$\eT^p(K)$ as given in \eqref{eq:dimqt .}}
elements.sent-2411-412: Deriving approximation properties for embedded Trefftz spaces is a nontrivial and problem-dependent task; see, e.g.,\cite{Lozinski_2019,LLSV_ARXIV_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-413: In this section, we present some numerical experiments in $(1 + 1)$ and $(2+1)$ dimensions to validate the theoretical results and assess numerically some additional features of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-414: The LDG scheme has been implemented using NGSolve \cite{ngsolve and NGSTrefftz~\cite{ngstrefftz .\footnote{Reproduction material is available in \cite{gomez_2024_14191529 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-415: In all the numerical experiments below, the stabilization parameter~$\eta^{\star}$ is set to~$10^{-1}$ and the weight parameter~$\alpha_F$ is set to~$1/2$ for all the time-like facets.
elements.sent-2411-416: We denote by~$\MT,\, \D,\, B$, and~$\Su$ the matrices associated with the bilinear forms~$\mh(\cdot, \cdot)$,\ $\bdh(\cdot, \cdot)$,\ $\bh(\cdot, \cdot)$,\ $\suh(\cdot, \cdot)$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-417: We also denote by~$\bq,\, \bu$ the vectors associated with the linear functionals~$\lqh(\cdot)$ and~$\luh(\cdot)$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-418: To do so, we consider the model problem~\eqref{EQN::MODEL-PROBLEM in~$(1 + 1)$ dimensions with homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions~$(\gD = 0)$ and~$\bk = 1$ on the space--time domain~$\QT = (0, 1)^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-419: We compute the 2-condition number of the stiffness matrix defined in Remark~\ref{REM::TIME-SLABS (which in this case is the same for all time slabs) for a sequence of meshes with uniform distributions along the space and time directions and~$\hKt = \hKx = 2^{-i}$, $i = 0, \dots, 6$, and uniform polynomial degrees~$p = 2, 3, 4$.}
elements.sent-2411-420: The results are shown in Figure~\ref{fig::cond , where we observe an asymptotic growing behavior of order~$\mathcal{O (h^{-1 )$ for all four discrete spaces and polynomial degrees.}}}

elements.sent-2411-422: We have employed Legendre bases for the tensor-product space.
elements.sent-2411-423: Since the use of monomial bases for standard and quasi-Trefftz polynomials leads to ill-conditioned stiffness matrices, we applied a Gram--Schmidt orthogonalization procedure to improve their conditioning.
elements.sent-2411-424: As for the embedded Trefftz space, the orthogonal embedding used in its construction ensures that the condition number cannot exceed that for the underlying polynomial space (see~\cite[Lemma~1]{LS_IJMNE_2023 ).}
